text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 54-year-old female with history of FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, bilateral, status post primary debulking surgery (total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, omentectomy, peritoneal stripping) achieving complete gross resection (R0), followed by adjuvant carboplatin/paclitaxel x 6 cycles, and currently on niraparib maintenance therapy. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant carrier. Presenting for routine oncologic surveillance, approximately 24 months following completion of platinum-based chemotherapy. Comparison CT from approximately 12 months prior available for review.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed following the intravenous administration of iodinated contrast material (non-ionic, weight-based dosing), with acquisition in the portal venous phase. No oral contrast was administered per departmental protocol. Images reconstructed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes at 3 mm slice thickness. DLP within institutional diagnostic reference levels.

---

FINDINGS:

LOWER CHEST (included for evaluation):
The imaged lung bases are clear. No focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or new pleural thickening. The included cardiac silhouette is normal in size and configuration. Incidentally noted is a small hiatal hernia, unchanged from prior examination.

LIVER AND BILIARY SYSTEM:
The liver is normal in size with smooth contour. Hepatic parenchymal attenuation is homogeneous throughout the portal venous phase without discrete hypo- or hyperattenuating lesions identified. The hepatic surface is smooth and free of nodular contour irregularity — specifically, no surface implants or peritoneal studding are seen overlying the hepatic dome or along the hepatoduodenal ligament, areas previously notable for surface involvement at initial presentation. The intrahepatic biliary ducts are not dilated. The gallbladder is present, without wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, or intraluminal calculi. The common bile duct is within normal caliber.

SPLEEN:
The spleen is normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation. No splenic lesions. No perisplenic fluid or soft tissue nodularity.

PANCREAS:
The pancreas is visualized in its entirety and is normal in morphology and parenchymal enhancement. No ductal dilation. No peripancreatic fluid collection or soft tissue stranding.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands appear normal in size and configuration. No adrenal masses or thickening.

KIDNEYS AND URETERS:
Both kidneys demonstrate normal size, parenchymal thickness, and cortical enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No renal calculi or cortical lesions. The ureters are not dilated. No perinephric fluid.

BLADDER:
The urinary bladder contains a moderate amount of urine with smooth bladder wall. No intravesical masses, wall irregularity, or perivesical soft tissue nodularity.

GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT:
The visualized stomach, small bowel, and large bowel are normal in caliber and wall thickness. No evidence of bowel obstruction, mesenteric tethering, or serosal irregularity. No mesenteric soft tissue deposits or confluent mesenteric fat stranding. The appendix was surgically resected (appendectomy as part of prior debulking surgery; no appendiceal remnant identified, consistent with prior operative history).

PELVIS:
The uterus and bilateral adnexa are surgically absent, consistent with prior total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. The surgical bed appears unremarkable, with expected post-operative fibrous changes and minor peritoneal thickening at the vaginal cuff. No pelvic soft tissue masses. No pelvic sidewall involvement. No cul-de-sac nodularity or soft tissue deposits in the rectouterine or rectovesical spaces. No pelvic free fluid.

PERITONEUM AND OMENTUM:
No peritoneal implants identified. The greater omentum is surgically absent (prior omentectomy); the anticipated omental remnant region and infracolonic mesentery are free of nodular deposits, fat stranding, or cake-like soft tissue confluent with the anterior abdominal wall. No ascites. The paracolic gutters bilaterally are clean, without fluid or soft tissue nodularity. The subphrenic spaces and subhepatic spaces are free of peritoneal disease.

DIAPHRAGM:
Bilateral hemidiaphragms are smooth in contour. No diaphragmatic surface nodularity, thickening, or serosal implants. Right hemidiaphragm region, previously the site of peritoneal stripping, demonstrates expected post-surgical fibrous change without nodular recurrence.

LYMPH NODES:
No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the retroperitoneum (aortocaval, para-aortic, or paracaval regions), mesentery, bilateral iliac chains, or inguinal regions. Scattered tiny sub-centimeter lymph nodes are seen in the retroperitoneum, all within expected normal limits and unchanged from prior study.

BONY STRUCTURES AND SOFT TISSUES:
No aggressive-appearing osseous lesions. No lytic or blastic foci to suggest metastatic involvement. Visualized lumbar vertebral bodies are intact. Anterior abdominal wall is intact at the prior midline laparotomy scar site; no herniation or wound-related complications identified. No significant soft tissue abnormality.

VASCULAR STRUCTURES:
Aorta is normal in caliber throughout the imaged segments. No significant atherosclerotic disease. Inferior vena cava is normal in caliber. Portal and splenic veins are patent and normally opacified.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No radiographic evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis, intra-abdominal disease recurrence, or new metastatic lesions. The peritoneal surfaces, diaphragm, paracolic gutters, and pelvic peritoneum are clean and unremarkable.

2. No ascites.

3. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the abdomen or pelvis.

4. The hepatic surface and parenchyma are free of lesions; no surface implants identified at the hepatic dome, which was a prior site of disease.

5. Post-surgical changes are stable and consistent with prior total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, and peritoneal/diaphragmatic stripping, without evidence of local recurrence at any surgical site.

6. Overall, these findings are consistent with sustained complete radiographic remission at the 24-month post-chemotherapy surveillance interval. No new or recurrent findings of concern identified compared to the prior study.

Signed: Attending Radiologist, Abdominal Imaging Division
